text:
In this paper, we introduce geo-indistinguishability as a formal privacy notion for location-based systems (LBS) ensuring the privacy of exact coordinates of a user while enabling the release of the imprecise locations needed to provide a service. The geo-indistinguishability notion is a generalization of differential privacy adapted to the Euclidean metric. Roughly, it offers a level of privacy proportional to a given radius r; that is, an unauthorized server will not be able to tell whether the user is at an exact location or at some other nearby location within the specified radius, while being able to tell the difference between larger locations such as cities.

Existing methods for achieving geo-indistinguishability apply noise sampled from the planar Laplace distribution. Discretization and truncation are addressed as a means of dealing with the digital grid and finite areas, and existing methods for location-obfuscation are compared to the proposed method. An application to a popular point-of-interest search service is presented with insights into possible implementations in terms of resource consumption and impact on communication throughput and service quality.
explanation: The text uses standard technical language and a generic abstract structure with no distinctive authorial cues, which is common in AI-generated summaries but can also come from real papers. The balance of general concepts (privacy, DP, Laplace) without specific results leans slightly toward AI generation.
